company.name: Public Storage
company.description: Public Storage is a self-storage REIT that owns and operates self-storage facilities in the United States and holds a major interest in European self-storage operator Shurgard.

overview.title: Business footprint
paragraphs: Public Storage is one of the largest owners and operators of self-storage facilities, with its 2025 annual report describing interests in 3,171 self-storage facilities across 40 U.S. states and about 229.4 million net rentable square feet.
The company also has international exposure through its equity interest in Shurgard Self Storage, a European self-storage operator.

overview.title: Revenue model
paragraphs: The core business rents storage units to households and businesses, with operating performance driven by facility occupancy, rent per occupied square foot, location density, property taxes, and acquisition or development activity.
The model benefits from local real-estate scarcity and scale, but demand is still tied to household moves, business inventory needs, promotional pricing, and the availability of local substitutes.

moatNarrative: Public Storage's moat is primarily physical and local: a large owned facility network, recognizable brand, operating systems, access to capital, and dense market coverage make it hard for a small operator to match its convenience or advertising reach.
The moat is not software-like. Storage units are relatively standardized, customers can switch at lease boundaries, and local supply additions or price promotions can pressure occupancy and rates.
decentralizationNarrative: Self-storage is already geographically distributed, but ownership and pricing are centralized around facility operators. Decentralized pressure is more likely to come from neighborhood-scale sharing, cooperative storage, better open mapping of available space, and inventory software that helps households or small businesses use existing space more efficiently.
Because the product is physical real estate with access control, insurance, and liability constraints, decentralization is plausible but operationally slower than in purely digital markets.
company.snapshotNote: Queued from the May 25, 2026 S&P 500 market-cap snapshot ranks 176-200.

moat.rationale: Large owned facility count, national brand presence, capital access, and dense local coverage support a strong physical moat, though individual storage units remain substitutable.

decentralizability.rationale: Storage demand can be partially decentralized through peer-to-peer space sharing, cooperatives, open mapping, and self-hosted inventory tooling, but access control, insurance, zoning, and trust constraints keep the core facility business hard to fully decentralize.

profitability.rationale: The 2025 annual report shows a large scaled REIT with substantial rental revenue and ongoing same-store operating metrics, consistent with a profitable mature property platform.

peRatio.rationale: REIT valuation is better interpreted through funds from operations than simple earnings, so the approximate P/E is included only as a rough public-market valuation signal.

marketCap.rationale: StockAnalysis reported Public Storage market capitalization of about $53.73 billion as of May 22, 2026.

products.name: Self-storage units
products.summary: Public Storage rents individual storage units at physical facilities for household goods, moving overflow, seasonal items, and small business inventory.
products.whyItMatters: Self-storage turns local real estate, access control, and convenience into a recurring rental product, making it a useful test case for whether physical-world capacity can be coordinated outside a large centralized property owner.
replacementSketch: A decentralized replacement would not need to copy the REIT footprint. It could combine neighborhood hosts, cooperative micro-warehouses, open local inventory maps, escrowed payments, and standardized access or insurance rules.

The near-term replacement path is likely partial: households and small businesses use open tools to identify, catalog, and share underused space before purpose-built community storage networks become credible at scale.

alternatives.name: OpenStreetMap
alternatives.summary: OpenStreetMap provides open, community-maintained geospatial data that can support discovery of local storage, warehouse, and access points without relying on a proprietary facility directory.

openness.rationale: OpenStreetMap data is openly licensed and community editable.

decentralizationFit.rationale: Open mapping improves discovery for many small operators or hosts, but it does not itself provide access control, payments, insurance, or dispute resolution.

readiness.rationale: The mapping layer is mature and widely used, though self-storage-specific coverage and metadata quality vary by locality.

costLeverage.rationale: Open map data can reduce discovery and listing costs for local alternatives, but physical storage costs remain dominated by real estate and operations.

disruptionConcepts.name: Neighborhood storage marketplace
disruptionConcepts.summary: A peer-to-peer storage network could let households, small businesses, churches, clubs, and local warehouses list unused garages, basements, lockers, or spare rooms under standardized terms, with open maps for discovery and reputation records for hosts and renters.
disruptionConcepts.thesis: The concept shifts storage supply from purpose-built centralized facilities toward underused local space, pressuring operators whose edge depends on location density and search visibility.
disruptionConcepts.bitcoinOrDecentralizationRole: Decentralization matters through multi-host supply, portable reputation, and open local discovery. Bitcoin or Lightning could be useful for escrow and small recurring payments, but it is not required for the core mechanism.
disruptionConcepts.coordinationMechanism: Renters search an open local directory, hosts publish space attributes and rules, and marketplace clients coordinate booking, deposits, access windows, and reviews.
disruptionConcepts.verificationOrTrustModel: Listings would need host identity checks, geotagged or time-stamped photos, renter deposits, signed condition reports, dispute evidence, and reputation that is portable across clients. Fraud is constrained by escrow release rules and repeat-host reputation, but not eliminated.
failureModes: Insurance, zoning, fire safety, and liability rules may make informal storage hard to scale.
Hosts can misrepresent space conditions or deny access, while renters can store prohibited goods or damage property.
Fragmented supply may be less convenient than a professionally operated facility with standardized access hours.
adoptionPath: Start with low-risk storage categories such as boxes, seasonal goods, and business overflow in communities with high storage prices.
Add standardized access logs, insurance templates, deposits, and dispute workflows before expanding into higher-value inventory.

decentralizationFit.rationale: The model directly distributes supply across many local hosts instead of a single facility owner.

coordinationCredibility.rationale: Marketplace coordination is credible in principle, but storage introduces higher trust, access, and liability burdens than many local services.

implementationFeasibility.rationale: Open maps, payment rails, and reputation systems are feasible, but insurance, safety, and dispute handling would require careful local execution.

incumbentPressure.rationale: The concept could pressure high-price urban and suburban markets, but it is unlikely to replace professionally managed facilities for customers needing predictable access and security.

disruptionConcepts.name: Cooperative micro-storage
disruptionConcepts.summary: Neighborhood associations, small businesses, or local cooperatives could operate shared micro-storage rooms or containerized storage nodes using open inventory software, shared governance, and transparent pricing instead of a national REIT model.
disruptionConcepts.thesis: The concept changes the market structure by replacing some commercial storage demand with member-owned or community-operated capacity that reuses existing buildings, containers, or underutilized rooms.
disruptionConcepts.bitcoinOrDecentralizationRole: The decentralization role is cooperative ownership and local governance. Bitcoin or Lightning could support transparent member payments, but the stronger mechanism is shared control of physical capacity.
disruptionConcepts.coordinationMechanism: Members pool capital or dues, reserve storage capacity through shared software, elect operators, and publish rules for access, maintenance, prohibited items, and surplus capacity.
disruptionConcepts.verificationOrTrustModel: Access logs, member identity, physical key control, inventory records, periodic audits, and transparent cooperative accounts reduce abuse. The model still depends on trusted local operators and enforceable membership rules.
failureModes: Volunteer or cooperative management may struggle with security, maintenance, collections, and disputes.
Small sites may lack climate control, loading access, or insurance coverage comparable to professional facilities.
Local governance can be captured by a small group or fail if member demand is seasonal.
adoptionPath: Begin with community organizations that already manage shared space, such as maker spaces, housing co-ops, churches, and neighborhood business associations.
Use open inventory and reservation tools to prove utilization before investing in dedicated shared storage rooms or containerized nodes.

decentralizationFit.rationale: Member-owned storage nodes decentralize ownership and governance, though each node remains locally administered.

coordinationCredibility.rationale: Cooperative coordination is credible for bounded communities where trust and repeated interaction already exist.

implementationFeasibility.rationale: The basic software and governance pieces are available, but physical security, insurance, and site management remain non-trivial.

incumbentPressure.rationale: This can reduce demand in specific communities and business niches, but it is unlikely to match the geographic breadth and convenience of Public Storage at national scale.

products.name: Business storage
products.summary: Public Storage markets storage units for small businesses that need space for inventory, equipment, documents, tools, or seasonal materials.
products.whyItMatters: Business storage is where self-storage overlaps with local logistics, inventory management, and small-business resilience, making it more exposed to open tools that help firms coordinate space and assets without renting from a large operator.
replacementSketch: A practical replacement can begin with better self-hosted asset tracking and reservation software for small businesses, tool libraries, maker spaces, and local merchants.
Over time, shared neighborhood warehousing or cooperative inventory pools could reduce the need for each business to rent a separate centralized unit.

alternatives.name: Shelf.nu
alternatives.summary: Shelf.nu is an open-source equipment and asset management system that can help organizations track physical items, locations, custody, and scheduling.

openness.rationale: The project is published as open-source software with a public GitHub repository.

decentralizationFit.rationale: Self-hosted asset tracking decentralizes operational data but does not by itself create shared physical storage capacity.

readiness.rationale: The project appears usable for asset tracking and scheduling, though storage-facility workflows would need adaptation.

costLeverage.rationale: Better inventory visibility can reduce redundant storage and misplaced equipment, but it does not eliminate real-estate costs where extra space is genuinely needed.

alternatives.name: Stowage
alternatives.summary: Stowage is a self-hosted open-source asset management system for small teams that want to track items without vendor lock-in.

openness.rationale: Stowage presents itself as free, open-source, self-hosted software.

decentralizationFit.rationale: Self-hosting gives small teams custody over their inventory records, but the software is an operational substitute rather than a full physical-space substitute.

readiness.rationale: The project is directly relevant to asset tracking, though it is not a purpose-built self-storage marketplace or facility system.

costLeverage.rationale: Inventory discipline can lower storage waste for small teams, but savings depend on whether poor tracking is actually causing excess space demand.

disruptionConcepts.name: Local business inventory coordination
disruptionConcepts.summary: Small businesses in the same district could coordinate inventory overflow, equipment lending, and storage reservations through open asset software and shared local spaces, reducing the need for each firm to rent separate commercial units.
disruptionConcepts.thesis: The concept pressures business storage demand by replacing isolated paid units with shared visibility, pooled capacity, and cooperative scheduling among businesses that already operate near each other.
disruptionConcepts.bitcoinOrDecentralizationRole: The central mechanism is decentralized coordination among local businesses. Lightning payments could settle small reservation fees or deposits, but the main value is shared custody and transparent inventory state.
disruptionConcepts.coordinationMechanism: Businesses publish available space, equipment, and storage needs to a shared local system; members reserve capacity, log item custody, and settle dues or usage fees under association rules.
disruptionConcepts.verificationOrTrustModel: Item check-in records, photos, barcodes or QR labels, access logs, member attestations, and deposit rules reduce false claims. The weakest point is still physical custody when goods are stored by another member.
failureModes: Businesses may be reluctant to expose inventory needs or share custody of valuable goods.
Loss, damage, theft, and insurance disputes could overwhelm informal local governance.
The model works best in dense business districts and may not scale in dispersed suburban markets.
adoptionPath: Start with low-value shared equipment, seasonal displays, and packaging materials among trusted local merchants.
Expand to formal shared storage rooms once reservation logs, custody records, and dispute processes are proven.

decentralizationFit.rationale: The model coordinates many local businesses rather than routing all overflow storage through a centralized REIT facility.

coordinationCredibility.rationale: Business associations and repeated local relationships give the model a credible starting trust network, but custody and liability remain hard.

implementationFeasibility.rationale: Open inventory tools can support the software layer, while physical access, insurance, and governance are the main implementation burdens.

incumbentPressure.rationale: The concept can reduce marginal demand from small businesses but will not replace professionally managed storage for high-value or access-sensitive inventory.

summaryMarkdown: Prepared a new Public Storage bundle with two products, sourced operating context from the 2025 10-K, and focused disruption analysis on peer-to-peer neighborhood storage, cooperative micro-storage, and open inventory coordination rather than forcing a Bitcoin-native thesis.
